disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Unresponsive with very slow, shallow breathing and blue lips (cyanosis)
trigger_question_examples: Is he breathing, and how does it look — fast, slow, shallow?
Are his lips or face turning blue?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's barely breathing, really slow and shallow, and his lips look blue.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Empty pill bottles/blister packs found nearby, known opioid use disorder
trigger_question_examples: Are there any pill bottles or drugs nearby?
Does he have a history of drug use?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, there are empty pill packets around him, and he has struggled with opioid use before.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Complete unresponsiveness to voice or shaking
trigger_question_examples: Does he respond at all if you call his name or shake him?
Have you tried a sternal rub or pinching to see if he responds?

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Does the household have a take-home naloxone kit
trigger_question_examples: Do you have a naloxone kit at home, like Nyxoid or Prenoxad?
Has he ever been prescribed a take-home naloxone kit before?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, actually we do have a naloxone nasal spray kit at home.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: [Parent] What do I do, he's barely breathing?!
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should confirm 000 has been called, instruct rolling the patient into the recovery position if breathing (to protect the airway), and if a take-home naloxone kit is available, instruct immediate administration following the kit's instructions while continuing to monitor breathing; should advise that rescue breaths may be needed if breathing stops, and should warn that naloxone's effects wear off before many opioids do, so re-sedation and repeat dosing (or CPR if breathing/pulse stops) may be needed while awaiting paramedics.
